title: Ervick, a Native of Prince Regents Bay
description: Portrait of Ervick, whole-length standing, facing the viewer, bearded, wearing fur clothing, and boots, icy landscape on the background; illustration to Ross' 'A Voyage of Discovery' (London: Murray, 1819). Aquatint and etching, hand-coloured.
creator: Daniel Havell
comments: Lettered above the image: "Artic Highlander", and below with title continuing: "Lat. 76. 12. N. _ Long. 65. W.", production details and publication line: "Captn J. Ross Delt / D. HAvell Scuplt / London, Published Jany 1819 by l. Murray, Albemarle Street".

description: Snow knife; carved of ivory; snow-knife with flat ivory blade attached to bone handle with twisted fibre cord.
creator: Eskimo-Aleut
acquisition_notes: Af/Am/Eu/As/Oc1921,1014.1 to 176 come from the historic ethnographic collection in the museum of the Yorkshire Philosophical Society, founded in 1822, of which a large part was purchased by the British Museum in 1921 for £250. The Register records, when known, the name of the donor and the date of the gift to the Society. This knife was given to the Philosophical Society by Captain Henry Hoppner.

description: Sledge made of bone, ivory, sealskin, wood, sinew, with wooden dog whip
creator: Polar Inuit (Inughuit)
materials: bone; ivory; seal skin; sinew; wood
acquisition_notes: This is the only object from Sir John Ross's first Arctic expedition to remain in the BM. The rest seem to have been specimens of natural history that later departed to South Kensington.
comments:
From Emma Vitale who studied the sled in 2024, paper published with co-author Bjarne Grønnow, Of Skin and Bones: Revisiting an Inughuit Dogsled Procured by John Ross in 1818:
The exhibited sled is not identical to the one shown in Ross’s account. Our investigation revealed that the sled consists of 89 different components made from whale and walrus bones, caribou antler, and a few pieces of wood. Furthermore, we discovered a previously undiscovered artefact; a dog whip mounted on the sled. The whip is made from local wood, features a finely shaped lash, and has a distinctive "knob" at the base end. These findings highlight the Inughuit people's technological ingenuity and strategic use of available materials during a period of isolation.
dimensions: Height: 70 centimetres Length: 145 Centimetres Width: 57 centimetres
current_location: On display (Room 26 - North America, Display Case 10)
